[[File:Uchihara training center of volunteer army of youth to cultivate Manchuria.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所]] [[File:Barracks of sun type in Uchihara training center.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所の[[日輪兵舎]]]] [[File:満蒙開拓団 寧安訓練所.png|thumb|300px|[[寧安]]訓練所。第一次満州開拓青年義勇隊の先遣部隊680名は、1938年4月21日に寧安と伊拉哈の両訓練所に入植した<ref>『徒歩旅行 : 体位向上』日本国際観光局満洲支部、1940年、p38</ref>]] '''満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍'''（まんもうかいたくせいしょうねんぎゆうぐん）とは、日本内地の数え年16歳から19歳の青少年を[[満州国]]に開拓民として送出する制度であり、[[満蒙開拓団]]に代表される[[満蒙開拓移民|満蒙開拓民]]送出事業の後半の主要形態である<ref name="araragi493">蘭（2012年）493ページ</ref>。
== 前史 == [[1932年]]（昭和7年）の満州国の建国から敗戦時に至るまで、一貫して「満州」（現中国東北部、以下「」を略す）への日本人農業移民事業の主導権を[[関東軍]]が握っていた<ref name="asada77">浅田（1993年）77ページ</ref><ref name="asada80">浅田（1993年）80ページ</ref>。この満州移民事業の展開は、以下の三期に分かれる<ref name="asada80"/>。 {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- !通番 !! 区分 !! 年代 !! 説明 |- !1 |試験移民期 || 1932年-1936年 || 日本人農業移民が満州で定着しうるかをためす時期 |- !2 |本格的移民期 || 1936年-1941年 || 日本人農業移民が日本政府の国策として満州に大量に送出された時期 |- !3 |移民事業崩壊期 || 1942年-1945年 || 日本人農業移民の満州への送出が停滞し、ついには全面停止に至る時期 |- |} 関東軍は、上述試験移民期にも満州大量移民計画案を作成し、その実施を日本政府に要請し続けていたが、日本政府とくに大蔵省の受け入れるところとならなかった<ref name="asada82">浅田（1993年）82ページ</ref>。しかし、[[1936年]]（昭和11年）の[[二・二六事件]]発生によって、軍部の政治的発言力が飛躍的に増大し、関東軍と陸軍省作成の満州大量移民計画を実施する絶好の機会となった<ref name="asada82"/>。同年には、「[[満州農業移民百万戸移住計画]]」が策定され、それが[[廣田内閣]]による七大国策の一つとして確定した「[[二十カ年百万戸送出計画]]」という壮大な計画も立てられるようになった（上掲表中の「本格的移民期」参照）<ref name="asada82"/>。しかし、翌[[1937年]]（昭和12年）に[[日中戦争]]が勃発し、戦争への大量動員と戦時景気に伴う労働力需要により、成人移民を多量に確保することが困難となっていた<ref name="araragi494">蘭（2012年）494ページ</ref>。
== 沿革 == [[1937年]]（昭和12年）11月3日「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍編成に関する建白書」という文章が首相[[近衛文麿]]をはじめ全閣僚に提出された<ref name="tsutsui193">筒井（1997年）193ページ</ref>。署名人は、農村更生協会理事長[[石黒忠篤]]、満州移住協会理事長[[大蔵公望]]、同理事[[橋本伝左衛門]]、[[那須皓]]、[[加藤完治]]ら6名であった<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民が蹉跌をきたしていた[[拓務省]]としては、この建白書は「渡りに船」であった。早速「満州に対する青少年移民送出に関する件」を立案し、11月30日の閣議でこれを決定している<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。またその年の12月には、「満州青年移民実施要項」を作成した<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。
== 義勇軍の募集 == 翌[[1938年]]（昭和13年）1月、この「満州青年移民実施要項」に基づいて、早々と募集が開始された<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。募集要項によると、小学校を卒業し、数え年16歳から19歳までの身体強健なる男子で、父母の承諾を得たものであれば誰でもよいとされた<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民を補充するものでありながら、その名称が青少年移民でなく、青少年義勇軍であるのは、日中戦争遂行上必要不可欠な満州支配の安定的維持に青少年が挺身することとして、当時軍国主義的意識の昂揚した青少年に訴えるためであった<ref name="araragi494"/>。その狙いが功を奏して、成人移民は貧農層が中心だったのに対して、青少年義勇軍は高等小学校の成績上位・中位層が中心となった<ref name="araragi494"/>。自由応募が原則であったが、実態は当局から各都道府県への割り当て数が決められ、さらに道府県から各学校への割り当て数が決められていた<ref name="araragi494"/>。それに応じて各高等小学校の担当教師が卒業生に主体的に応募するように働きかけた<ref name="araragi494"/>。青少年義勇軍送出において学校教育の果たした役割は重要であった<ref name="araragi494"/>。 [[1941年]]（昭和16年）に[[大日本青少年団]]が結成されると、県によっては青少年団の地方組織を活用して満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍への参加を促す例も見られた<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.i-manabi.jp/system/regionals/regionals/ecode:2/66/view/8353 |title=愛媛県史 近代 下（昭和63年２月29日発行） |publisher=愛媛県生涯学習センター |date=1988年 |accessdate=2022-05-08}}</ref>。
== 入植まで == 各都道府県で選抜された青少年300名を標準として中隊に組織し、加藤完治が所長を務める茨城県内原の満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍訓練所（いわゆる内原訓練所）で3か月の学習、武道及び体育と農作業の基礎訓練を受けた後に、満州国の現地訓練所にて3ヵ年の訓練を経て、義勇隊開拓団として入植した<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>梶山（1983年）7ページ</ref>。この訓練教育期間は、満州国における「民族協和」の中核として開拓地の満州国の発展、さらには日満一体化に寄与することが期待された<ref name="araragi494"/>。
また、義勇軍周知も兼ねたイベントとして、 [[1939年]]（昭和14年）[[6月7日]]には[[朝日新聞社]]主催による「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍壮行会」が[[明治神宮外苑競技場]]で開催された<ref>神宮外苑協議場で壮行会『朝日新聞』（昭和14年6月8日）『昭和ニュース辞典第7巻 昭和14年-昭和16年』p742 昭和ニュース事典編纂委員会 毎日コミュニケーションズ刊 1994年</ref>。
== 入植の実態 == この満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍は、1938年（昭和13年）から1945年（昭和20年）の敗戦までの8カ年の間に8万6,000人の青少年が送りだされた<ref name="araragi494"/>。これは満州開拓民送出事業総体の人員の3割を占めており、同事業に欠かせない存在であったといえる<ref name="araragi494"/>。しかし、その実態をみると、青少年だけで構成されているだけに、団幹部の力量に左右される面があった<ref name="araragi494"/>。また、その入植地の環境も一般開拓団以上に厳しい場合も多かった<ref name="araragi494"/>。そうした中で一致団結して理想を追求した団もあったが、条件が悪く、しかも団幹部に恵まれない場合には、精神的に耐えられず生活が荒んだ者もあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。暴力事件や周辺農村との間で軋轢を起こすこともあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。

[[File:Uchihara training center of volunteer army of youth to cultivate Manchuria.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所]] [[File:Barracks of sun type in Uchihara training center.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所の[[日輪兵舎]]]] [[File:満蒙開拓団 寧安訓練所.png|thumb|300px|[[寧安]]訓練所。第一次満洲開拓青年義勇隊の先遣部隊680名は、1938年4月21日に寧安と伊拉哈の両訓練所に入植した<ref>『徒歩旅行 : 体位向上』日本国際観光局満洲支部、1940年、p38</ref>]] '''満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍'''（まんもうかいたくせいしょうねんぎゆうぐん）とは、日本内地の数え年16歳から19歳の青少年を[[満洲国]]に開拓民として送出する制度であり、[[満蒙開拓団]]に代表される[[満蒙開拓移民|満蒙開拓民]]送出事業の後半の主要形態である<ref name="araragi493">蘭（2012年）493ページ</ref>。
== 前史 == [[1932年]]（昭和7年）の満洲国の建国から敗戦時に至るまで、一貫して「満洲」（現中国東北部、以下「」を略す）への日本人農業移民事業の主導権を[[関東軍]]が握っていた<ref name="asada77">浅田（1993年）77ページ</ref><ref name="asada80">浅田（1993年）80ページ</ref>。この満洲移民事業の展開は、以下の三期に分かれる<ref name="asada80"/>。 {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- !通番 !! 区分 !! 年代 !! 説明 |- !1 |試験移民期 || 1932年-1936年 || 日本人農業移民が満洲で定着しうるかをためす時期 |- !2 |本格的移民期 || 1936年-1941年 || 日本人農業移民が日本政府の国策として満洲に大量に送出された時期 |- !3 |移民事業崩壊期 || 1942年-1945年 || 日本人農業移民の満洲への送出が停滞し、ついには全面停止に至る時期 |- |} 関東軍は、上述試験移民期にも満洲大量移民計画案を作成し、その実施を日本政府に要請し続けていたが、日本政府とくに大蔵省の受け入れるところとならなかった<ref name="asada82">浅田（1993年）82ページ</ref>。しかし、[[1936年]]（昭和11年）の[[二・二六事件]]発生によって、軍部の政治的発言力が飛躍的に増大し、関東軍と陸軍省作成の満洲大量移民計画を実施する絶好の機会となった<ref name="asada82"/>。同年には、「[[満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画]]」が策定され、それが[[廣田内閣]]による七大国策の一つとして確定した「[[二十カ年百万戸送出計画]]」という壮大な計画も立てられるようになった（上掲表中の「本格的移民期」参照）<ref name="asada82"/>。しかし、翌[[1937年]]（昭和12年）に[[日中戦争]]が勃発し、戦争への大量動員と戦時景気に伴う労働力需要により、成人移民を多量に確保することが困難となっていた<ref name="araragi494">蘭（2012年）494ページ</ref>。
== 沿革 == [[1937年]]（昭和12年）11月3日「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍編成に関する建白書」という文章が首相[[近衛文麿]]をはじめ全閣僚に提出された<ref name="tsutsui193">筒井（1997年）193ページ</ref>。署名人は、農村更生協会理事長[[石黒忠篤]]、満洲移住協会理事長[[大蔵公望]]、同理事[[橋本伝左衛門]]、[[那須皓]]、[[加藤完治]]ら6名であった<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民が蹉跌をきたしていた[[拓務省]]としては、この建白書は「渡りに船」であった。早速「満洲に対する青少年移民送出に関する件」を立案し、11月30日の閣議でこれを決定している<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。またその年の12月には、「満洲青年移民実施要項」を作成した<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。
== 義勇軍の募集 == 翌[[1938年]]（昭和13年）1月、この「満洲青年移民実施要項」に基づいて、早々と募集が開始された<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。募集要項によると、小学校を卒業し、数え年16歳から19歳までの身体強健なる男子で、父母の承諾を得たものであれば誰でもよいとされた<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民を補充するものでありながら、その名称が青少年移民でなく、青少年義勇軍であるのは、日中戦争遂行上必要不可欠な満洲支配の安定的維持に青少年が挺身することとして、当時軍国主義的意識の昂揚した青少年に訴えるためであった<ref name="araragi494"/>。その狙いが功を奏して、成人移民は貧農層が中心だったのに対して、青少年義勇軍は高等小学校の成績上位・中位層が中心となった<ref name="araragi494"/>。自由応募が原則であったが、実態は当局から各都道府県への割り当て数が決められ、さらに道府県から各学校への割り当て数が決められていた<ref name="araragi494"/>。それに応じて各高等小学校の担当教師が卒業生に主体的に応募するように働きかけた<ref name="araragi494"/>。青少年義勇軍送出において学校教育の果たした役割は重要であった<ref name="araragi494"/>。 [[1941年]]（昭和16年）に[[大日本青少年団]]が結成されると、県によっては青少年団の地方組織を活用して満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍への参加を促す例も見られた<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.i-manabi.jp/system/regionals/regionals/ecode:2/66/view/8353 |title=愛媛県史 近代 下（昭和63年２月29日発行） |publisher=愛媛県生涯学習センター |date=1988年 |accessdate=2022-05-08}}</ref>。
== 入植まで == 各都道府県で選抜された青少年300名を標準として中隊に組織し、加藤完治が所長を務める茨城県内原の満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍訓練所（いわゆる内原訓練所）で3か月の学習、武道及び体育と農作業の基礎訓練を受けた後に、満洲国の現地訓練所にて3ヵ年の訓練を経て、義勇隊開拓団として入植した<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>梶山（1983年）7ページ</ref>。この訓練教育期間は、満洲国における「民族協和」の中核として開拓地の満洲国の発展、さらには日満一体化に寄与することが期待された<ref name="araragi494"/>。
また、義勇軍周知も兼ねたイベントとして、 [[1939年]]（昭和14年）[[6月7日]]には[[朝日新聞社]]主催による「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍壮行会」が[[明治神宮外苑競技場]]で開催された<ref>神宮外苑協議場で壮行会『朝日新聞』（昭和14年6月8日）『昭和ニュース辞典第7巻 昭和14年-昭和16年』p742 昭和ニュース事典編纂委員会 毎日コミュニケーションズ刊 1994年</ref>。
== 入植の実態 == この満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍は、1938年（昭和13年）から1945年（昭和20年）の敗戦までの8カ年の間に8万6,000人の青少年が送りだされた<ref name="araragi494"/>。これは満洲開拓民送出事業総体の人員の3割を占めており、同事業に欠かせない存在であったといえる<ref name="araragi494"/>。しかし、その実態をみると、青少年だけで構成されているだけに、団幹部の力量に左右される面があった<ref name="araragi494"/>。また、その入植地の環境も一般開拓団以上に厳しい場合も多かった<ref name="araragi494"/>。そうした中で一致団結して理想を追求した団もあったが、条件が悪く、しかも団幹部に恵まれない場合には、精神的に耐えられず生活が荒んだ者もあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。暴力事件や周辺農村との間で軋轢を起こすこともあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 蘭信三「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」[[貴志俊彦]]・[[松重充浩]]・松村史紀編『二〇世紀満洲歴史事典』吉川弘文館、2012年 （平成24年） 12月10日 第一刷発行、ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4、493-494頁。 * 「岩波講座 近代日本と植民地（第3巻）植民地化と産業化」（1993年）[[岩波書店]]所収浅田喬二「満州農業移民と農業・土地問題」 * 筒井五郎「鉄道自警村-私説・満州移民史-」（1997年）日本図書刊行会 * 梶山盛夫「私のなかの満州-義勇隊訓練所と戦跡-」（1983年）主婦の友出版サービスセンター * 上笙一郎「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」（1973年）中央公論社
== 関連書 == * 『遥かなり望郷の軌跡：伊拉哈義勇隊原中隊回顧録』第五次伊拉哈会、1984.8 * 『秋田県満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍外史』 後藤和雄、無明舎出版、2014.10
== 外部リンク == ; 体験談 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/07/H_07_218_1.pdf 私の歩み]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/03/H_03_036_1.pdf 満蒙開拓少年隊]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/03/H_03_125_1.pdf 思い出の逃避行]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 ; 画像 * [http://www.rekishi-archive.city.naha.okinawa.jp/archives/item3/41182 海外での体験／満蒙開拓少年義勇軍、寧安訓練所を巡視中の警備隊]那覇市歴史博物館 ; 論文 * 内木靖, 「[https://doi.org/10.15088/00000329 満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍--その生活の実態]」『愛知県立大学大学院国際文化研究科論集』 第11巻 p.79-108 2010年, 愛知県立大学 * 今井良一, 「[https://doi.org/10.9747/jars.16.2_20 「満洲」開拓青年義勇隊派遣の論理とその混成中隊における農業訓練の破綻]」『村落社会研究ジャーナル』 16巻 2号 2010年 p.20-32, 日本村落研究学会, {{doi|10.9747/jars.16.2_20}}。 * 白取道博, 「[https://hdl.handle.net/2115/29341 「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」の変容（1938～1941年）：「郷土部隊編成」導入の意義]」『北海道大學教育學部紀要』 54巻 p.33-96, 1990-02, 北海道大學教育學部 * 白取道博, 「[https://hdl.handle.net/2115/29282 「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」の創設過程]」『北海道大學教育學部紀要』 45巻 p.189-222 1985-12, 北海道大學教育學部

[[File:Uchihara training center of volunteer army of youth to cultivate Manchuria.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所]] [[File:Barracks of sun type in Uchihara training center.jpg|thumb|300px|内原訓練所の[[日輪兵舎]]]] [[File:満蒙開拓団 寧安訓練所.png|thumb|300px|[[寧安]]訓練所。第一次満洲開拓青年義勇隊の先遣部隊680名は、1938年4月21日に寧安と伊拉哈の両訓練所に入植した<ref>『徒歩旅行 : 体位向上』日本国際観光局満洲支部、1940年、p38</ref>]] '''満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍'''（まんもうかいたくせいしょうねんぎゆうぐん）とは、日本内地の数え年16歳から19歳の青少年を[[満洲国]]に開拓民として送出する制度であり、[[満蒙開拓団]]に代表される[[満蒙開拓移民|満蒙開拓民]]送出事業の後半の主要形態である<ref name="araragi493">蘭（2012年）493ページ</ref>。

== 入植の実態 == この満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍は、1938年（昭和13年）から1945年（昭和20年）の敗戦までの8カ年の間に8万6,000人の青少年が送りだされた<ref name="araragi494"/>。これは満洲開拓民送出事業総体の人員の3割を占めており、同事業に欠かせない存在であったといえる<ref name="araragi494"/>。しかし、その実態をみると、青少年だけで構成されているだけに、団幹部の力量に左右される面があった<ref name="araragi494"/>。また、その入植地の環境も一般開拓団以上に厳しい場合も多かった<ref name="araragi494"/>。そうした中で一致団結して理想を追求した団もあったが、条件が悪く、しかも団幹部に恵まれない場合には、精神的に耐えられず生活が荒んだ者もあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。暴力事件や周辺農村との間で軋轢を起こすこともあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 蘭信三「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」[[貴志俊彦]]・[[松重充浩]]・松村史紀編『二〇世紀満洲歴史事典』吉川弘文館、2012年 （平成24年） 12月10日 第一刷発行、ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4、493-494頁。 * 「岩波講座 近代日本と植民地（第3巻）植民地化と産業化」（1993年）[[岩波書店]]所収浅田喬二「満州農業移民と農業・土地問題」 * 筒井五郎「鉄道自警村-私説・満州移民史-」（1997年）日本図書刊行会 * 梶山盛夫「私のなかの満州-義勇隊訓練所と戦跡-」（1983年）主婦の友出版サービスセンター * 上笙一郎「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」（1973年）中央公論社
== 関連書 == * 『遥かなり望郷の軌跡：伊拉哈義勇隊原中隊回顧録』第五次伊拉哈会、1984.8 * 『秋田県満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍外史』 後藤和雄、無明舎出版、2014.10

revision.text:
'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Manmo Kaitaku Seishonen Giyugun}} [[Category:Historical human migration]] [[Category:Manchuria]] [[Category:Japanese colonial policies]] [[Category:Inner Manchuria]] [[Category:History of Manchuria]] [[Category:Prewar Shōwa-era colonial policies]] [[Category:Shōwa-era diplomacy]] [[Category:Japan–China relations]] [[Category:Colonialism]] [[Category:East Asian history]] [[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]

== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策}}

'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>

Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>

The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). == Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). '''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策}}

'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>

The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>

Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

{{DEFAULTSORT:Manmo Kaitaku Seishonen Giyugun}} [[Category:Historical human migration]] [[Category:Manchuria]] [[Category:Japanese colonial policies]] [[Category:Inner Manchuria]] [[Category:History of Manchuria]] [[Category:Prewar Shōwa-era colonial policies]] [[Category:Shōwa-era diplomacy]] [[Category:Japan–China relations]] [[Category:Colonialism]] [[Category:East Asian history]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]

'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

revision.comment: 画像位置移動。本文記事量に比較して画像サイズが若干大きいので、サイズを縮小。+photo
revision.text:
'''満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍'''（まんもうかいたくせいしょうねんぎゆうぐん）とは、日本内地の数え年16歳から19歳の青少年を[[満洲国]]に開拓民として送出する制度であり、[[満蒙開拓団]]に代表される[[満蒙開拓移民|満蒙開拓民]]送出事業の後半の主要形態である<ref name="araragi493">蘭（2012年）493ページ</ref>。
== 前史 == [[File:Uchihara training center of volunteer army of youth to cultivate Manchuria.jpg|thumb|250px|内原訓練所]] [[File:Barracks of sun type in Uchihara training center.jpg|thumb|250px|内原訓練所の[[日輪兵舎]]]] [[1932年]]（昭和7年）の満洲国の建国から敗戦時に至るまで、一貫して「満洲」（現中国東北部、以下「」を略す）への日本人農業移民事業の主導権を[[関東軍]]が握っていた<ref name="asada77">浅田（1993年）77ページ</ref><ref name="asada80">浅田（1993年）80ページ</ref>。この満洲移民事業の展開は、以下の三期に分かれる<ref name="asada80"/>。 {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- !通番 !! 区分 !! 年代 !! 説明 |- !1 |試験移民期 || 1932年-1936年 || 日本人農業移民が満洲で定着しうるかをためす時期 |- !2 |本格的移民期 || 1936年-1941年 || 日本人農業移民が日本政府の国策として満洲に大量に送出された時期 |- !3 |移民事業崩壊期 || 1942年-1945年 || 日本人農業移民の満洲への送出が停滞し、ついには全面停止に至る時期 |- |} 関東軍は、上述試験移民期にも満洲大量移民計画案を作成し、その実施を日本政府に要請し続けていたが、日本政府とくに大蔵省の受け入れるところとならなかった<ref name="asada82">浅田（1993年）82ページ</ref>。しかし、[[1936年]]（昭和11年）の[[二・二六事件]]発生によって、軍部の政治的発言力が飛躍的に増大し、関東軍と陸軍省作成の満洲大量移民計画を実施する絶好の機会となった<ref name="asada82"/>。同年には、「[[満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画]]」が策定され、それが[[廣田内閣]]による七大国策の一つとして確定した「[[二十カ年百万戸送出計画]]」という壮大な計画も立てられるようになった（上掲表中の「本格的移民期」参照）<ref name="asada82"/>。しかし、翌[[1937年]]（昭和12年）に[[日中戦争]]が勃発し、戦争への大量動員と戦時景気に伴う労働力需要により、成人移民を多量に確保することが困難となっていた<ref name="araragi494">蘭（2012年）494ページ</ref>。
== 沿革 == [[1937年]]（昭和12年）11月3日「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍編成に関する建白書」という文章が首相[[近衛文麿]]をはじめ全閣僚に提出された<ref name="tsutsui193">筒井（1997年）193ページ</ref>。署名人は、農村更生協会理事長[[石黒忠篤]]、満洲移住協会理事長[[大蔵公望]]、同理事[[橋本伝左衛門]]、[[那須皓]]、[[加藤完治]]ら6名であった<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民が蹉跌をきたしていた[[拓務省]]としては、この建白書は「渡りに船」であった。早速「満洲に対する青少年移民送出に関する件」を立案し、11月30日の閣議でこれを決定している<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。またその年の12月には、「満洲青年移民実施要項」を作成した<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。
== 義勇軍の募集 == [[File:満蒙開拓団 寧安訓練所.png|thumb|250px|[[寧安]]訓練所。第一次満洲開拓青年義勇隊の先遣部隊680名は、1938年4月21日に寧安と伊拉哈の両訓練所に入植した<ref>『徒歩旅行 : 体位向上』日本国際観光局満洲支部、1940年、p38</ref>]] 翌[[1938年]]（昭和13年）1月、この「満洲青年移民実施要項」に基づいて、早々と募集が開始された<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。募集要項によると、小学校を卒業し、数え年16歳から19歳までの身体強健なる男子で、父母の承諾を得たものであれば誰でもよいとされた<ref name="tsutsui193"/>。成人移民を補充するものでありながら、その名称が青少年移民でなく、青少年義勇軍であるのは、日中戦争遂行上必要不可欠な満洲支配の安定的維持に青少年が挺身することとして、当時軍国主義的意識の昂揚した青少年に訴えるためであった<ref name="araragi494"/>。その狙いが功を奏して、成人移民は貧農層が中心だったのに対して、青少年義勇軍は高等小学校の成績上位・中位層が中心となった<ref name="araragi494"/>。自由応募が原則であったが、実態は当局から各都道府県への割り当て数が決められ、さらに道府県から各学校への割り当て数が決められていた<ref name="araragi494"/>。それに応じて各高等小学校の担当教師が卒業生に主体的に応募するように働きかけた<ref name="araragi494"/>。青少年義勇軍送出において学校教育の果たした役割は重要であった<ref name="araragi494"/>。 [[1941年]]（昭和16年）に[[大日本青少年団]]が結成されると、県によっては青少年団の地方組織を活用して満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍への参加を促す例も見られた<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.i-manabi.jp/system/regionals/regionals/ecode:2/66/view/8353 |title=愛媛県史 近代 下（昭和63年２月29日発行） |publisher=愛媛県生涯学習センター |date=1988年 |accessdate=2022-05-08}}</ref>。
== 入植まで == [[File:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍 - 8 June 1939 - 光芸社.png|thumb|250px|1939年6月8日の壮行会の様子。[[数寄屋橋]]交差点にあった旧朝日新聞社前。]] 各都道府県で選抜された青少年300名を標準として中隊に組織し、加藤完治が所長を務める茨城県内原の満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍訓練所（いわゆる内原訓練所）で3か月の学習、武道及び体育と農作業の基礎訓練を受けた後に、満洲国の現地訓練所にて3ヵ年の訓練を経て、義勇隊開拓団として入植した<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>梶山（1983年）7ページ</ref>。この訓練教育期間は、満洲国における「民族協和」の中核として開拓地の満洲国の発展、さらには日満一体化に寄与することが期待された<ref name="araragi494"/>。
また、義勇軍周知も兼ねたイベントとして、 [[1939年]]（昭和14年）[[6月7日]]には[[朝日新聞社]]主催による「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍壮行会」が[[明治神宮外苑競技場]]で開催された<ref>神宮外苑協議場で壮行会『朝日新聞』（昭和14年6月8日）『昭和ニュース辞典第7巻 昭和14年-昭和16年』p742 昭和ニュース事典編纂委員会 毎日コミュニケーションズ刊 1994年</ref>。
== 入植の実態 == この満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍は、1938年（昭和13年）から1945年（昭和20年）の敗戦までの8カ年の間に8万6,000人の青少年が送りだされた<ref name="araragi494"/>。これは満洲開拓民送出事業総体の人員の3割を占めており、同事業に欠かせない存在であったといえる<ref name="araragi494"/>。しかし、その実態をみると、青少年だけで構成されているだけに、団幹部の力量に左右される面があった<ref name="araragi494"/>。また、その入植地の環境も一般開拓団以上に厳しい場合も多かった<ref name="araragi494"/>。そうした中で一致団結して理想を追求した団もあったが、条件が悪く、しかも団幹部に恵まれない場合には、精神的に耐えられず生活が荒んだ者もあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。暴力事件や周辺農村との間で軋轢を起こすこともあった<ref name="araragi494"/>。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 蘭信三「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」[[貴志俊彦]]・[[松重充浩]]・松村史紀編『二〇世紀満洲歴史事典』吉川弘文館、2012年 （平成24年） 12月10日 第一刷発行、ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4、493-494頁。 * 「岩波講座 近代日本と植民地（第3巻）植民地化と産業化」（1993年）[[岩波書店]]所収浅田喬二「満州農業移民と農業・土地問題」 * 筒井五郎「鉄道自警村-私説・満州移民史-」（1997年）日本図書刊行会 * 梶山盛夫「私のなかの満州-義勇隊訓練所と戦跡-」（1983年）主婦の友出版サービスセンター * 上笙一郎「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」（1973年）中央公論社
== 関連書 == * 『遥かなり望郷の軌跡：伊拉哈義勇隊原中隊回顧録』第五次伊拉哈会、1984.8 * 『秋田県満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍外史』 後藤和雄、無明舎出版、2014.10
== 外部リンク == ; 体験談 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/07/H_07_218_1.pdf 私の歩み]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/03/H_03_036_1.pdf 満蒙開拓少年隊]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.heiwakinen.go.jp/wp-content/uploads/archive/library/roukunote/hikiage/03/H_03_125_1.pdf 思い出の逃避行]}} 平和祈念展示資料館 ; 画像 * [http://www.rekishi-archive.city.naha.okinawa.jp/archives/item3/41182 海外での体験／満蒙開拓少年義勇軍、寧安訓練所を巡視中の警備隊]那覇市歴史博物館 ; 論文 * 内木靖, 「[https://doi.org/10.15088/00000329 満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍--その生活の実態]」『愛知県立大学大学院国際文化研究科論集』 第11巻 p.79-108 2010年, 愛知県立大学 * 今井良一, 「[https://doi.org/10.9747/jars.16.2_20 「満洲」開拓青年義勇隊派遣の論理とその混成中隊における農業訓練の破綻]」『村落社会研究ジャーナル』 16巻 2号 2010年 p.20-32, 日本村落研究学会, {{doi|10.9747/jars.16.2_20}}。 * 白取道博, 「[https://hdl.handle.net/2115/29341 「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」の変容（1938～1941年）：「郷土部隊編成」導入の意義]」『北海道大學教育學部紀要』 54巻 p.33-96, 1990-02, 北海道大學教育學部 * 白取道博, 「[https://hdl.handle.net/2115/29282 「満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍」の創設過程]」『北海道大學教育學部紀要』 45巻 p.189-222 1985-12, 北海道大學教育學部

revision.text:
'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Manmō settlement projects|ja|満蒙開拓移民}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} in [[1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[1945]], the {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|ja|満洲}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Manmo Kaitaku Seishonen Giyugun}} [[Category:Historical human migration]] [[Category:Manchuria]] [[Category:Japanese colonial policies]] [[Category:Inner Manchuria]] [[Category:History of Manchuria]] [[Category:Prewar Shōwa-era colonial policies]] [[Category:Shōwa-era diplomacy]] [[Category:Japan–China relations]] [[Category:Colonialism]] [[Category:East Asian history]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[Category:Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]

Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 Incident|ja|二・二六事件}} in [[1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal opportunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|ja|廣田内閣}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|ja|日中戦争}} in [[1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|ja|近衛文麿}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was approved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> Applicants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to supplement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary applications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to apply.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|ja|関東軍}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|ja|満洲国}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策}}
