== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|qq=|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] * [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] * [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24895653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]], as part of the later stage of the [[:en:Japanese settlers in Manchuria|Manmō settlement projects]] represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] * [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24895653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]], as part of the later stage of the [[:en:Japanese settlers in Manchuria|Manmō settlement projects]] represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] * [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

<!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} [[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{wikidata link|Q24895653}} [[Category:Colonialism]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Historical migrations]] [[Category:History of East Asia]] [[Category:History of Japan–China Relations]] [[Category:History of Manchuria]] [[Category:Inner Manchuria]] [[Category:Japanese Colonial Policies]] [[Category:Japanese military occupations]] [[Category:Manchuria]] [[Category:Prewar Diplomacy of the Shōwa Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24895653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]], as part of the later stage of the [[:en:Japanese settlers in Manchuria|Manmō settlement projects]] represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>

Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] * [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|version=103478774|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]
<!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--><!--Manshū-Mō Kaitaku Seishonen Giyūgun--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍}} [[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{wikidata link|Q24895653}} [[Category:Colonialism]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Historical migrations]] [[Category:History of East Asia]] [[Category:History of Japan–China Relations]] [[Category:History of Manchuria]] [[Category:Inner Manchuria]] [[Category:Japanese Colonial Policies]] [[Category:Japanese military occupations]] [[Category:Manchuria]] [[Category:Prewar Diplomacy of the Shōwa Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24895653]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]], as part of the later stage of the [[:en:Japanese settlers in Manchuria|Manmō settlement projects]] represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of [[:en:Manchukuo|Manchukuo]] in [[:en:1932|1932]] until Japan's defeat in [[:en:1945|1945]], the [[:en:Kwantung Army|Kwantung Army]] consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in [[:en:Manchuria|Manchuria]] (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the [[:en:February 26 incident|February 26 Incident]] in [[:en:1936|1936]] greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the [[:en:Hirota Cabinet|Hirota Cabinet]], leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the [[:en:Second Sino-Japanese War|Second Sino-Japanese War]] in [[:en:1937|1937]] led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister [[:en:Fumimaro Konoe|Fumimaro Konoe]] and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January [[:en:1938|1938]], recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 incident|en|February 26 incident|WD=Q477958|lt=February 26 Incident}} in {{ill|1936|en|1936|WD=Q18649|lt=1936}} greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|en|Hirota Cabinet|WD=Q11486424|lt=Hirota Cabinet}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q170314|lt=Second Sino-Japanese War}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}} led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January {{ill|1938|en|1938|WD=Q18645|lt=1938}}, recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.

'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Japanese settlers in Manchuria|en|Japanese settlers in Manchuria|WD=Q6334864|lt=Manmō settlement projects}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932}} until Japan's defeat in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}}, the {{ill|Kwantung Army|en|Kwantung Army|WD=Q646394|lt=Kwantung Army}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|WD=Q81126|lt=Manchuria}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 incident|en|February 26 incident|WD=Q477958|lt=February 26 Incident}} in {{ill|1936|en|1936|WD=Q18649|lt=1936}} greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|en|Hirota Cabinet|WD=Q11486424|lt=Hirota Cabinet}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q170314|lt=Second Sino-Japanese War}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}} led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]][[zh:满蒙开拓青少年义勇军]]
'''Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun''' (満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍) refers to the system of sending Japanese youths aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning) from the Japanese mainland as settlers to {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}}, as part of the later stage of the {{ill|Japanese settlers in Manchuria|en|Japanese settlers in Manchuria|WD=Q6334864|lt=Manmō settlement projects}} represented by the {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}}.<ref name="araragi493">Araragi (2012), p. 493.</ref>
== Background == From the establishment of {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} in {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932}} until Japan's defeat in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}}, the {{ill|Kwantung Army|en|Kwantung Army|WD=Q646394|lt=Kwantung Army}} consistently controlled the migration project to settle Japanese agricultural immigrants in {{ill|Manchuria|en|Manchuria|WD=Q81126|lt=Manchuria}} (northeastern China, referred to as "Manchuria" below).<ref name="asada77">Asada (1993), p. 77.</ref><ref name="asada80">Asada (1993), p. 80.</ref> The development of this immigration project can be divided into three phases:<ref name="asada80"/> {|class="wikitable" style="font-size:small" |- ! Phase !! Period !! Description |- ! 1 | Experimental Immigration Phase || 1932–1936 || Testing the feasibility of Japanese agricultural settlers establishing themselves in Manchuria. |- ! 2 | Full-Scale Immigration Phase || 1936–1941 || Large-scale Japanese agricultural immigration implemented as a national policy. |- ! 3 | Collapse of the Immigration Project || 1942–1945 || Immigration to Manchuria stagnated, eventually ceasing entirely. |- |} During the experimental phase, the Kwantung Army drafted large-scale immigration plans for Manchuria and repeatedly requested their implementation by the Japanese government, but these proposals, particularly due to opposition from the Ministry of Finance, were not accepted.<ref name="asada82">Asada (1993), p. 82.</ref> However, the {{ill|February 26 incident|en|February 26 incident|WD=Q477958|lt=February 26 Incident}} in {{ill|1936|en|1936|WD=Q18649|lt=1936}} greatly enhanced the military's political influence, creating an ideal o11ortunity to implement the immigration plans formulated by the Kwantung Army and the Army Ministry.<ref name="asada82"/> That same year, the {{ill|Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|ja|満洲農業移民百万戸移住計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|1=Plan for the Settlement of One Million Farming Households in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17210858}} was formulated and incorporated as one of the seven major national policies by the {{ill|Hirota Cabinet|en|Hirota Cabinet|WD=Q11486424|lt=Hirota Cabinet}}, leading to the grand {{ill|20-Year Plan for One Million Families|ja|二十カ年百万戸送出計画|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/20-Year Plan for One Million Families|qq=}}.<ref name="asada82"/> However, the outbreak of the {{ill|Second Sino-Japanese War|en|Second Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q170314|lt=Second Sino-Japanese War}} in {{ill|1937|en|1937|WD=Q18647|lt=1937}} led to widespread military mobilization and increased demand for labor, making it difficult to secure sufficient adult immigrants.<ref name="araragi494">Araragi (2012), p. 494.</ref>
== History == On November 3, 1937, a proposal titled "Memorial on the Formation of the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army" was submitted to Prime Minister {{ill|Fumimaro Konoe|en|Fumimaro Konoe|WD=Q315594|lt=Fumimaro Konoe}} and all cabinet members.<ref name="tsutsui193">Tsutsui (1997), p. 193.</ref> The signatories included prominent figures such as Ishiguro Tadatsugu, President of the Rural Rehabilitation Association; Ōkura Kimomo, President of the Manchurian Immigration Association; and others.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> The Ministry of Colonial Affairs welcomed this proposal, quickly drafting a plan for "Youth Migration to Manchuria," which was a11roved at a cabinet meeting on November 30.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> By December, the "Implementation Guidelines for Manchurian Youth Migration" were established.<ref name="tsutsui193"/>
== Recruitment == In January {{ill|1938|en|1938|WD=Q18645|lt=1938}}, recruitment for the Manmō Youth Volunteer Army began based on the implementation guidelines.<ref name="tsutsui193"/> A11licants had to be male, aged 16 to 19 (by traditional age reckoning), in good physical condition, and have parental consent. The program aimed to su11lement adult immigrants, but its name emphasized "volunteer army" rather than "youth migration" to align with the militaristic and nationalistic sentiments of the time.<ref name="araragi494"/> This strategy successfully attracted a higher proportion of academically above-average students compared to adult immigrants, who were mainly from impoverished farming families.<ref name="araragi494"/> Although voluntary a11lications were emphasized, recruitment was managed through quotas assigned by local governments to schools.<ref name="araragi494"/> Teachers played a crucial role in encouraging students to a11ly.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== Training and Settlement == Recruited youths were organized into units of about 300 individuals and trained at the {{ill|Uchihara Training Center|ja|内原訓練所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchihara Training Center|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Ibaraki Prefecture, where they underwent a three-month program of martial arts, physical training, and basic farming techniques.<ref name="araragi494"/> Afterward, they received three years of further training at local facilities in Manchukuo before being sent to settlements as part of the volunteer army.<ref name="araragi494"/><ref>Kajiyama (1983), p. 7.</ref>
== Actual Conditions == Between 1938 and the end of the war in 1945, a total of 86,000 youths were sent to Manchuria, comprising about 30% of the total Japanese settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> The success of settlements often depended on the competence of their leaders and local conditions, which could be harsher than those faced by general settlers.<ref name="araragi494"/> Some groups achieved their ideals through unity, while others struggled with poor conditions and leadership, leading to discontent, violence, and conflicts with surrounding communities.<ref name="araragi494"/>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Araragi, Shinzo. "Manmō Kaitaku Seishōnen Giyūgun." In Toshiaki Kishi, Mitsuhiro Matsushige, and Fuminori Matsumura (Eds.), *20th-Century Manchurian Historical Encyclopedia*. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2012. ISBN 978-4-642-01469-4. * Asada, Kyōji. "Japanese Agricultural Immigration to Manchuria." In *Iwanami Lecture Series: Modern Japan and Its Colonies* (Vol. 3). Iwanami Shoten, 1993. * Tsutsui, Gorō. *Tetsudō Jikeimura: My History of Manchurian Immigration*. Nihon Tosho Kankōkai, 1997. * Kajiyama, Morio. *Manchuria in Me: Volunteer Corps Training Center and War Sites*. Shufu no Tomo Publishing, 1983.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Manmō Kaitakudan|ja|満蒙開拓団|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manmō Kaitakudan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 満蒙開拓移民|1=Japanese settlers in Manchuria|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6334864}} * {{ill|Kwantung Army|en|Kwantung Army|WD=Q646394|lt=Kwantung Army}} * {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} * {{ill|Japanese Colonial Policy|ja|日本の植民政策|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Colonial Policy|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{translated page|ja|満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍|version=103478774|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:満蒙開拓青少年義勇軍]]
